areaTag.zh: 家事法 · 子女 · 临时 · 药检

one.zh: 养育（临时）——子女自 2020 年 12 月分居以来一直随母生活、仅在监督下见父；西澳儿童保护部门将子女带离母亲照顾。父亲的程序申请被驳回。
one.en: Parenting (interim) — the child had lived with the mother and saw the father only on supervised time since separation in December 2020; the child was removed from the mother's care by the WA child-protection authority. The father's application was dismissed.

issue.zh: 在一宗漫长的临时养育诉讼中，父亲（把诉求收窄后）申请命令母亲接受毛囊药检；法院是否应依 s 102NJ(1)(c) 作出该命令？以及随之而来的费用应如何处理？
issue.en: In long-running interim parenting litigation the father (having narrowed his application) sought an order that the mother undergo hair-follicle drug testing; should the court make that order under s 102NJ(1)(c)? And how should costs be dealt with?

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – INTERIM – Where the child has lived with the mother and spent only supervised time with the father since the parties' separation in December 2020 – Where the child was removed from the mother's care by the Western Australian child protection authority.

subseq.zh: 本案为系列第 7 号判决；本资料库未见进一步记录。
subseq.en: This is the seventh decision in the series; no further record in this library.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

t.zh: 故事 · 全本（涵盖全案）

html.zh:
这是一对悬殊的伴侣：父亲 Mr Yoxall 生于 1960 年（66 岁），是 F Pty Ltd 的创始人；母亲 Ms Eide 生于 1987 年（38 岁），是一名医护人员，周均收入约 1,800 澳元。两人于 2020 年 12 月分居，育有一女 X（2018 年生，8 岁，就读 AS School 三年级）。

分居以来，父亲从未与女儿单独相处——一直只能在专业监督下探视。母亲于 2021 年 3 月 12 日提起实质性家事诉讼。这对当事人有一段极其漫长、反复的诉讼史，还在维多利亚州最高法院、郡法院、地方法院同时进行其他诉讼；实质性的财产与养育案归 Johns 法官审理。他们甚至曾花约三年，仅就「双方是否构成事实婚姻关系」这一门槛问题争讼；2024 年 5 月 16 日 Williams 法官依 s 90RD 宣告双方曾为事实婚姻关系。现行临时养育令由高级司法登记官 Hoult 于 2021 年 9 月 23 日作出：孩子随母生活，父亲在专业监督下定期探视。

2026 年 4 月，情况骤变：女儿被西澳儿童保护部门从母亲处带离一晚。父亲随即提出一份紧急临时申请，洋洋洒洒 29 项命令——包括改判孩子随他生活（母亲每周仅 3 小时监督探视）、或大幅增加他的无监督过夜时间、另做家庭报告、指定独立儿童律师、驳回母亲的财产分割申请，以及要求母亲接受毛囊药检。

然而到了开庭那天，父亲把所有诉求收窄成了唯一一项：要求母亲做毛囊（药物与酒精）检测。这正是法院要决定的全部。

依 s 102NJ(1)(c)，法院确有权命令一方在未经同意下接受药检。但父亲在整个诉讼中已多次提出同样要求，母亲一贯拒绝自愿受检、称毫无正当依据，且从未被法院命令受检。就在不久前（2025-12-09），Johns 法官在本系列判决中已处理过同一申请——她接受母亲陈述：父亲没有提出任何证据支持此命令；而母亲作为主要照顾者的地位本就不受质疑，既如此，实在难以理解为何还需要、或适合做这种检测。

Hartnett 法官审理本次申请，得出同样结论：证据并不支持作出毛囊检测的命令。结果——父亲的申请被驳回。

在费用上，母亲请求按 party-party 标准判付固定 8,765 澳元（并提交费用清单 Exhibit M3）；父亲对费用未作任何回应。法院指出：依 s 114UB(1) 一般规则各自负担费用，但依 s 114UB(2)，若存在正当理由，法院有裁量权判付费用——并援引 Mallet v Mallet、Penfold v Penfold 关于费用裁量的原则。最终，法院命令父亲在 60 天内向母亲支付 8,765 澳元费用。
html.en:
An unevenly-matched couple: the father, Mr Yoxall, was born in 1960 (66) and founded F Pty Ltd; the mother, Ms Eide, was born in 1987 (38) and is a health professional earning about $1,800 a week. They separated in December 2020 and have one daughter, X (born 2018, 8, in Grade Three at AS School).

Since separation the father has had no unsupervised time with the child — only professionally supervised time. The mother began the substantive proceedings on 12 March 2021. The parties have a very long, protracted litigation history, with concurrent matters in the Victorian Supreme, County and Magistrates Courts; the substantive property and parenting matters are docketed to Johns J. They even spent about three years litigating the threshold question of whether they were in a de facto relationship; on 16 May 2024 Williams J declared under s 90RD that they had been. The current interim parenting orders were made by Senior Judicial Registrar Hoult on 23 September 2021: the child lives with the mother and spends professionally supervised time with the father.

In April 2026 things changed sharply: the daughter was removed from the mother's care by the WA child-protection authorities for one night. The father filed an urgent interim application with some 29 wide-ranging orders — including that the child live with him (the mother to have only 3 hours' supervised time per week), or that he have substantially increased unsupervised overnight time, a further family report, an Independent Children's Lawyer, dismissal of the mother's property application, and an order that the mother undergo hair-follicle drug testing.

But by the hearing the father had narrowed everything to a single order: that the mother undergo hair-follicle (drug and alcohol) testing. That was all the court had to decide.

Under s 102NJ(1)(c) the court does have power to order testing without consent. But the father had repeatedly sought this throughout; the mother had consistently refused to test voluntarily, saying there was no proper basis, and had never been ordered to. Only recently (9 Dec 2025) Johns J had dealt with the same application in this series — accepting the mother's submission that the father adduced no evidence to support it, and that, since the mother's role as primary parent was not in challenge, it was difficult to see why such testing would be necessary or appropriate.

Hartnett J reached the same conclusion: the evidence did not support a hair-follicle-testing order. The father's application was dismissed.

On costs, the mother sought party-party costs fixed at $8,765 (tendering a costs table, Exhibit M3); the father made no submissions in response. The court noted that under s 114UB(1) the general rule is each party bears their own costs, but under s 114UB(2) the court may order costs where justifying circumstances exist — citing Mallet v Mallet and Penfold v Penfold on the costs discretion. The court ordered the father to pay the mother $8,765 within 60 days.

t.zh: 判决路径 · 案件走向

html.zh:
起点：临时申请父亲把 29 项诉求收窄为一项：命令母亲做毛囊检测
↓
① 权力来源 s 102NJ(1)(c)法院有权命令未经同意的药检
↓
② 证据门槛有无证据支持此项检测？
↓
③ 既有裁决（Johns J, No 6）无证据 · 母亲主要照顾地位不受质疑
↓
结论：证据不支持 → 驳回申请费用：依 s 114UB 父亲付母亲 8,765 澳元

此图展示法官的判断顺序：临时申请被收窄 → 确认 s 102NJ 的权力 → 审查证据门槛 → 援引同案既有认定 → 因证据不足驳回 → 处理费用。
html.en:
Start: interim applicationfather narrowed 29 orders to one: hair-follicle testing
↓
① Power · s 102NJ(1)(c)court may order testing without consent
↓
② Evidentiary thresholdis there evidence to support testing?
↓
③ Prior finding (Johns J, No 6)no evidence · primary-carer role not challenged
↓
Conclusion: not supported → dismissedcosts: under s 114UB, father pays mother $8,765

The judge's order of reasoning: the application is narrowed → confirm the s 102NJ power → test the evidentiary threshold → rely on the prior finding in the same series → dismiss for want of evidence → deal with costs.

§ s 114UB ›
§ s 90RD ›

html.zh:
⚖ Eide & Yoxall (No 6) ›
⚖ Mallet v Mallet ›
⚖ Penfold v Penfold ›
⚖ Fitzgerald v Fish ›
html.en:
⚖ Eide & Yoxall (No 6) ›
⚖ Mallet v Mallet ›
⚖ Penfold v Penfold ›
⚖ Fitzgerald v Fish ›

html.zh:
第 1 步 · 定性
“The issue for consideration before me is the father's application for the mother to undergo hair follicle testing.”
✎ CasePocket 解读

尽管父亲原本提了 29 项命令，开庭时只剩一项——要求母亲做毛囊检测。法院的任务因此被收窄到这一个问题。

第 2 步 · 权力来源
“Pursuant to s 102NJ(1)(c) of the Act, the Court has the power … for a party to undergo drug and/or alcohol testing without their consent.”
✎ CasePocket 解读

法院承认确有权命令未经同意的药检——但「有权力」不等于「应当作出」，关键看证据。

第 3 步 · 既有认定（Johns J, No 6）
“There is no evidence before the Court that would justify the making of that order … the mother's role as primary parent is not subject to challenge …”
✎ CasePocket 解读

就在几个月前，Johns 法官已就同一申请认定：没有证据、母亲主要照顾者地位不受质疑。Hartnett 法官援引并认同。

第 4 步 · 结论
（依 catchwords）“the evidence does not support a need for hair follicle testing”。
✎ CasePocket 解读

证据不足 → 父亲的申请被驳回。

第 5 步 · 费用
“Section 114UB(1) … each party shall bear their own costs … s 114UB(2) … such order as to costs as the Court considers just.”
✎ CasePocket 举例 · 理解 party-party 费用

一般规则是各自付费；但当一方反复提出已被否决、缺乏依据的申请，法院可认定存在「正当理由」判其付对方费用。本案父亲被判付母亲 8,765 澳元（60 天内）。（本例为 CasePocket 解读，非判决原文。）
html.en:
Step 1 · framing
“The issue for consideration before me is the father's application for the mother to undergo hair follicle testing.”
✎ CasePocket explainer

Though the father had filed 29 orders, by the hearing only one remained — that the mother undergo hair-follicle testing. The court's task narrowed to that single question.

Step 2 · the power
“Pursuant to s 102NJ(1)(c) of the Act, the Court has the power … for a party to undergo drug and/or alcohol testing without their consent.”
✎ CasePocket explainer

The court accepted it had the power to order testing without consent — but having the power is not the same as exercising it; that turns on the evidence.

Step 3 · the prior finding (Johns J, No 6)
“There is no evidence before the Court that would justify the making of that order … the mother's role as primary parent is not subject to challenge …”
✎ CasePocket explainer

Only months earlier Johns J had found, on the same application: no evidence, and the mother's primary-carer role not in challenge. Hartnett J relied on and agreed with those findings.

Step 4 · conclusion
(per the catchwords) “the evidence does not support a need for hair follicle testing”.
✎ CasePocket explainer

Insufficient evidence → the father's application was dismissed.

Step 5 · costs
“Section 114UB(1) … each party shall bear their own costs … s 114UB(2) … such order as to costs as the Court considers just.”
✎ CasePocket example · party-party costs

The general rule is each party pays their own costs; but where a party repeatedly brings an already-rejected, unsupported application, the court may find justifying circumstances and order them to pay the other side. Here the father was ordered to pay the mother $8,765 within 60 days. (This example is CasePocket's, not the judgment.)

t.zh: 本案确立什么 · 可援引于
t.en: What this case establishes · cite for

html.zh:
程序背景
FCFCOA Division 1 · 临时申请（interim）· Hartnett J；适用 2026 现行法。
判决理由 Ratio
法院依 s 102NJ 命令一方未经同意接受药检，须有证据基础；在申请人已将诉求收窄、且其主要照顾者地位不受质疑、又无任何支持证据时，应驳回该申请。
可援引于 Cite for
① 反对无证据基础的强制药检申请；② s 114UB 下的费用裁量；③ 临时申请被收窄后的处理。
实务要点 Practice
反复提出已被否决、缺乏证据的同类申请，可能构成判付对方费用的「正当理由」。
★ 学生考点
临时养育 + s 102NJ 检测命令的证据门槛 + s 114UB 费用——一个「权力 ≠ 应当行使」的范例。
适用边界
临时裁定、事实高度个案化；不决定实质养育争议（归 Johns J）。
html.en:
Procedural posture
FCFCOA Division 1 · interim application · Hartnett J; applies current (2026) law.
Ratio
An order under s 102NJ for a party to undergo testing without consent needs an evidentiary basis; where the applicant has narrowed the application, the primary-carer role is not in challenge, and there is no supporting evidence, the application should be dismissed.
Cite for
① resisting an evidence-free compulsory-testing application; ② the costs discretion under s 114UB; ③ handling an interim application that has been narrowed.
Practice points
Repeatedly bringing an already-rejected, unsupported application can amount to justifying circumstances for a costs order against that party.
★ Exam point
Interim parenting + the evidentiary threshold for an s 102NJ testing order + s 114UB costs — a power-not-equal-to-must-exercise example.
Limits
Interim, highly fact-specific; does not decide the substantive parenting dispute (docketed to Johns J).

html.zh:
Best interests of the child · 子女最佳利益
判定一切子女事项的首要标准。
Interim order · 临时令
诉讼进行中作出的暂时性命令。
Hair follicle testing · 毛囊检测
通过毛发样本检测药物/酒精使用情况。
Testing without consent (s 102NJ) · 未经同意的检测
依 s 102NJ(1)(c)，法院可命令一方在未经同意下接受药物/酒精检测。
Party-party costs · 按对方标准的费用
按规定费用表向对方支付的诉讼费用。
De facto relationship · 事实婚姻关系
未登记结婚但以伴侣身份共同生活；可依 s 90RD 宣告。
Independent Children's Lawyer · 独立儿童律师
法院指定、独立代表子女最佳利益的律师。
First instance · 初审
案件首次审理（非上诉）；具说服力而非约束力。
Ratio decidendi · 判决理由
判决据以成立的核心法律理由。
De-identified / pseudonym · 去识别化 / 化名
家事判决以化名发布以保护当事人身份（Part XIVB）。
html.en:
Best interests of the child
The paramount standard for all decisions about children.
Interim order
A temporary order made while proceedings are on foot.
Hair follicle testing
Drug/alcohol testing using a hair sample.
Testing without consent (s 102NJ)
Under s 102NJ(1)(c), the court may order a party to undergo drug/alcohol testing without consent.
Party-party costs
Litigation costs payable to the other party on the prescribed scale.
De facto relationship
A couple living together though not married; may be declared under s 90RD.
Independent Children's Lawyer
A lawyer appointed to independently represent the child's best interests.
First instance
The first hearing (not an appeal); persuasive, not binding.
Ratio decidendi
The core legal reason the decision rests on.
De-identified / pseudonym
Family-law judgments use pseudonyms to protect identities (Part XIVB).

sc.zh: 药物/酒精检测命令的权力

desc.zh: 法院有权就诉讼中产生的问题作出命令，包括命令一方在未经同意的情况下接受药物及/或酒精检测。
desc.en: The court may make an order on an issue arising in the proceedings, including for a party to undergo drug and/or alcohol testing without their consent.

src.zh: 判决原文：“Pursuant to s 102NJ(1)(c) of the Act, the Court has the power to make an order … for a party to undergo drug and/or alcohol testing without their consent.”
src.en: From the judgment: “Pursuant to s 102NJ(1)(c) of the Act, the Court has the power to make an order … for a party to undergo drug and/or alcohol testing without their consent.”

desc.zh: (1) 一般规则为各方自负费用；(2) 若法院认为存在正当理由，可作出其认为公正的费用命令。
desc.en: (1) The general rule is that each party bears their own costs; (2) where justifying circumstances exist, the court may make such order as to costs as it considers just.

src.zh: 判决原文：“Section 114UB(1) … each party shall bear their own costs … s 114UB(2) … power … to make such order as to costs as the Court considers just.”
src.en: From the judgment: “Section 114UB(1) … each party shall bear their own costs … s 114UB(2) … power … to make such order as to costs as the Court considers just.”

sc.en: Declaration of a de facto relationship

desc.zh: 法院可宣告双方是否曾构成事实婚姻关系——适用财产分割条文的门槛。
desc.en: The court may declare whether the parties were in a de facto relationship — a threshold to the property provisions.

src.zh: 判决：Williams J 依 s 90RD 宣告双方曾为事实婚姻关系。
src.en: From the judgment: Williams J made a declaration under s 90RD that the parties had been in a de facto relationship.

sc.zh: 事实婚姻关系下的财产分割
sc.en: Property division for de facto relationships

desc.zh: 事实婚姻关系下变更财产权益的命令；母亲据此提出财产申请（父亲曾求驳回）。
desc.en: Orders altering property interests in a de facto relationship; the mother's property application (which the father sought to dismiss) was brought under it.

src.zh: 判决：the mother's application for property division under s 90SM(3) …
src.en: From the judgment: the mother's application for property division under s 90SM(3) …

summary.zh: 本系列前案。Johns 法官已就同一「毛囊检测」申请作出处理。
summary.en: An earlier decision in this series. Johns J had already dealt with the same hair-follicle-testing application.

prop.zh: 无证据支持作出检测命令；母亲作为主要照顾者的地位不受质疑，难见检测之必要或适当。
prop.en: There was no evidence to justify a testing order; the mother's role as primary parent was not in challenge, so testing was neither necessary nor appropriate.

EideNo6.passage: Johns J ([18]-[22]): “There is no evidence before the Court that would justify the making of that order … The reality is that the mother's role as primary parent is not subject to challenge. It is difficult to understand why such testing would be necessary or appropriate given that circumstance.”

src.zh: Hartnett J 援引 Johns J 的上述认定，并得出相同结论。
src.en: Hartnett J relied on Johns J's findings above and reached the same conclusion.

summary.zh: 澳大利亚高等法院判例，就费用裁量等原则被引用。
summary.en: A High Court of Australia decision, cited on costs-discretion principles.

prop.zh: 费用裁量的原则（引用并讨论 Penfold v Penfold）。
prop.en: Principles on the exercise of the costs discretion (quoting and discussing Penfold v Penfold).

Mallet.passage: Footnote: “Penfold v Penfold (1980) FLC 90-800 … quoted in Mallet v Mallet [1984] HCA 21; (1984) FLC 91-507 at pp 79,123-79,124.”

src.zh: 就费用裁量在脚注被引用。
src.en: Cited in a footnote on the costs discretion.

summary.zh: 高等法院判例，关于费用裁量原则，被 Mallet 引用。
summary.en: A High Court decision on costs-discretion principles, quoted in Mallet.

prop.en: Principles on the exercise of the costs discretion.

summary.zh: 家事法院判例，作为援引权威之一。
summary.en: A Family Court of Australia decision, cited as authority.

prop.zh: （作为援引判例之一；与儿童代表/程序相关。）
prop.en: (One of the authorities cited; relating to child representation/procedure.)

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARTNETT J

PRELIMINARY

These reasons for judgment explain the orders made by me on 16 April 2026, and further the orders made by me this day.
The issue before the Court was contained in an Application in a Proceeding filed 15 April 2026 by the father, respondent in the substantive proceedings (“the father”). The father urgently sought a suite of parenting orders in respect of the parties’ child, X, born in 2018 (aged eight years), including that the child live with him after her removal from the mother’s care by the Department of Communities in April 2026.
The mother, applicant in the substantive proceedings (“the mother”), filed a Response to an Application in a Proceeding on 16 April 2026, in which she sought for the father’s application to be dismissed and for the father to pay her costs.
The matter came before me for urgent hearing on 16 April 2026. During the hearing, the father conceded that the child had been returned to the mother’s care by the Department of Communities on 13 April 2026. He abandoned the bulk of the orders sought in his application, and only pursued orders for the mother to undertake hair follicle testing for alcohol and substance use.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The father relied upon:
(1) his Application in a Proceeding filed 15 April 2026 (“the father’s application”);

(2) his affidavit filed 15 April 2026; and

(3) a video tendered in evidence (now marked Exhibit F1).

The mother relied upon:
(1) her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 16 April 2026;

(2) her affidavit filed 16 April 2026 (together with annexures);

(3) documents tendered in evidence in relation to ongoing Magistrates’ Court matters (Exhibits M1 and M2); and

(4) her table of party-party costs on scale dated 16 April 2026 (Exhibit M3); and

(5) the father’s Costs Notice filed 9 December 2025.

ORDERS SOUGHT

The father’s application sought some 29 wide-ranging orders, including, inter alia:

· for the parties to share decision-making responsibility;

· for the child to either:

· live with the father and spend three hours’ professionally supervised time per week with the mother; or

· live with the mother and spend substantial unsupervised overnight time with the father, building to five nights per fortnight during school term and a week about arrangement during school holidays;

· a family report (without any reference to the family report currently being written by Dr B);

· the appointment of an Independent Children’s Lawyer;

· for the mother to undertake hair follicle testing for drug and alcohol use;

· arrangements for communication between the parties; and

· for the mother’s application for property division under s 90SM(3) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) to be dismissed.

During the hearing, the father discontinued the bulk of his application and only sought orders for the mother to undertake hair follicle testing, as proposed in the following terms in his application:
Hair Follicle Tests to be Undertaken by the Applicant Mother
That within 24 hours the Applicant Mother shall make an appointment and attend for hair collection with [AV Company], [AW Company] or nominee for drug and alcohol hair follicle testing and for the purpose of this hair follicle testing:
(a) Collection is to be conducted by a qualified and certified collector.

(b) Chain of Custody procedure is to be applied to the sample.

(c) Testing is to be conducted at an approved laboratory, accredited to conduct hair drug testing to the recognised International Standard ISO/IEC 17025:2005 by the relevant national accreditation body for that laboratory.

(d) Either head or body may be collected for testing.

(e) The Applicant Mother is required to maintain her head hair at a length of not less than four (4) centimetres and shall not cut, bleach or dye her hair between the date of this order and the time of collection of hair.

(f) Each party or their legal representatives is at liberty to provide [AV Company], [AW Company] or nominee with a copy of these orders.

(g) The Applicant Mother shall provide the collector with photographic identification to be recorded before each hair collection and authority, with this order also hereby authorising [AV Company], [AW Company] or nominee to provide the results of each test to both parties, the father and the mother, and/or their legal representatives upon receipt of such test results.

(h) The hair drug and alcohol test screen for alcohol EtG and drugs of abuse including amphetamine-type substances, metabolites, cannabis and metabolites, cocaine and metabolites, opioids and metabolites and any other drug specified in this order.

The father’s application initially proposed for the mother to be responsible for the costs of the hair follicle testing. During the hearing, the father proposed that he pay for the testing.
The father submitted that the wide range of other orders initially sought, and I observe to be heard on an urgent basis, had been a “wishlist”.[1] He further indicated that, although not an issue before the Court, he “will be going for full custody straightaway” following the hearing.[2]
In her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 16 April 2026, the mother sought for the father’s application to be dismissed; the father to pay her costs; and any such further or other orders as the Court deems appropriate.
Prior to the hearing on 16 April 2026, the Court granted leave for the mother to be excused from attendance at the in-person hearing, provided her counsel appeared in person and was able to obtain her client’s instructions. In response to the father’s change in position, counsel for the mother submitted that the mother should not have to undergo hair follicle testing and that costs should be ordered against the father on a party-party basis, fixed in the sum of $8,765.

RELEVANT FACTUAL BACKGROUND

The father, Mr Yoxall, was born in 1960 and is 66 years of age. He is the founder of F Pty Ltd.
The mother, Ms Eide, was born in 1987 and is 38 years of age. She is a health professional and earns an average of $1,800 per week.
The parties’ child X was born in 2018. She is eight years of age and in Grade Three at AS School.
The parties separated in December 2020. The father has spent no unsupervised time with the child since the parties’ separation.
The mother commenced the substantive family law proceedings on 12 March 2021. The parties have a very long history of protracted family law litigation before this Court, with other matters running concurrently in the Victorian Supreme Court, County Court and Magistrates’ Court. The substantive financial and parenting proceedings are docketed to the Honourable Justice Johns.[3] Previously, the parties spent some three years in litigation over a threshold issue of whether they lived in a de facto relationship within the meaning of the Act. On 16 May 2024, the Honourable Justice Williams made a declaration pursuant to s 90RD of the Act, as sought by the mother, that the parties had been in a de facto relationship.[4]
The current interim parenting orders were made by Senior Judicial Registrar Hoult on 23 September 2021. Under this arrangement, the child lives with the mother and spends professionally supervised time with the father each Sunday between 2:00pm and 5:00pm, save for those times when the father is engaged in work-related travel and/or his health needs preclude his spending time with the child, as claimed by him. The child also communicates with the father via videocall each Wednesday evening between 6:30pm and 7:00pm. There are also operative parenting orders in relation to communication with the supervision service and the mother’s overseas travel made by Justice Johns on 20 November 2024 and 18 March 2025.
It was the mother’s evidence that she was diagnosed with Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (“PTSD”) by her psychiatrist, Dr AT, in relation to family violence perpetrated against her by the father during the parties’ relationship, including threats to kill, physical assaults and stalking. She further submitted that post-separation the father has perpetrated emotional and economic abuse against her, and is funding multiple proceedings against her, including:
(1) a trial in the Supreme Court issued against her by F Pty Ltd and listed to commence in mid-2026;

(2) County Court proceedings issued against her by F Pty Ltd;

(3) Magistrates’ Court proceedings issued against her by F Pty Ltd;

(4) Magistrates’ Court proceedings in relation to an application for an intervention order against the mother by the father’s employee or friend Mr LL; and

(5) a private prosecution issued against her by the father in the Magistrates’ Court also listed in mid-2026.

The father denies the mother’s allegations of family violence. I am not in a position to make findings in relation to these allegations.
It was the father’s evidence that the mother has a longstanding history of illicit substance use, excessive alcohol consumption and mental health challenges. His evidence in relation to this point is confined to allegations of:
(1) the mother experiencing issues with illicit substances between 2012 and 2019;

(2) the mother experiencing issues with alcohol use while pregnant;

(3) various statements made to the father in conversation by AS School staff;

(4) an interaction between the mother and police in 2020;

(5) a criminal charge against the mother in 2022; and

(6) an intervention order against the mother protecting a third party made in 2025.

The father provided no evidence in support of his allegations, beyond annexing to his affidavit alleged text messages between the parties in 2019. Broadly, the mother denied the father’s allegations or stated that there was insufficient information to comment on them. She denied that she has a longstanding history of illicit drug or alcohol consumption. She stated that although she drinks alcohol, she has only historically used illicit substances and does not have issues with substance abuse. She further noted that the intervention order the father refers to is that involving Mr LL referred to in her own material. Again, I am not in a position to make findings in relation to any of these allegations.

The events of April 2026

In April 2026, the mother and child attended Melbourne Airport to travel to Country AU for a holiday. The father had consented to this holiday, and the mother provided him with her flight details, itinerary and accommodation information. The father’s weekly supervised time with the child had also been rescheduled to the day before in April 2026 to accommodate for the holiday.
While attempting to check in, the mother and child were approached by a process server and served with Magistrates’ Court documents alleging criminal activity and a private prosecution against the mother, with the father listed as the informant. The mother’s solicitors were not contacted in relation to this prosecution. It was the mother’s evidence that she became significantly distressed, cried and believed that she was being arrested and charged by the police. Her attempts to call her solicitors were unsuccessful.
It was the mother’s evidence that during the flight, she was seated next to a passenger who acted in a way that she perceived to be unusual. At some point during the flight, the passenger left the seat and did not return. The mother’s evidence is that she became fearful that she was being monitored or harassed, or possibly that the person was an associate of the father. She became very agitated and distressed when the cabin crew did not explain where that person had gone, and refused to sit down. The flight was diverted in response to the mother’s behaviour.
The mother conceded that her behaviour during the flight was irrational. She submitted that she believed it was triggered by her PTSD and the shock of being served with criminal legal documents immediately before boarding. It was her evidence that she was not intoxicated and had not consumed illicit substances, and that she does not have issues with substance use. She did not know at the time that the flight was being diverted.
Upon landing, Australian Federal Police officers boarded the aircraft to remove and arrest the mother. The father tendered in evidence a video of a woman being arrested on an aircraft (now marked Exhibit F1).
The mother was held in custody overnight.
Following the mother’s arrest, the child was taken into the care of child protection authorities. The mother was unable to recommend any available caregiver for the child at the time.
At 9:51pm, the father received a phone call from a crisis worker at the child protection authorities, who advised that the mother had been arrested, handcuffed and forcibly removed from the aircraft. It was the father’s evidence that the worker asked him if he could travel to care for the child and that, due to issues with flights, he instead suggested a friend who could potentially care for the child until his arrival. It was further his evidence that a few hours later, he received another call from the child protection authorities and was informed that due to an intervention order against the father, they could not release the child into his care, and advised him to make an urgent application to this Court.

The events from the following day in April 2026 onwards

The mother was charged with offences in relation to alleged disorderly or offensive behaviour on an aircraft. She appeared at the Magistrates’ Court in April 2026 and was released on bail. She is next required to attend the Magistrates’ Court in May 2026.
After her release from custody, the mother contacted the child protection authorities and spoke with a case manager. She was informed that the child protection authorities were satisfied that the child could return to her care. The child was returned to the mother at her hotel at or around 3:00pm that day.
It was the father’s evidence that on the same day in April 2026 he received correspondence from the child protection authorities stating that they had assessed the child to be at immediate and substantial risk and stating that the child had been taken into provisional care.
The next day in April 2026, the child protection authorities sent email correspondence to the mother, confirming that they:[5]
(1) had taken the child into care due to the absence of a suitable caregiver;

(2) would not be progressing any application for ongoing care;

(3) supported the child returning to the mother’s care, with a safety plan in place while the mother and the child remained in that city; and

(4) intended to send an interstate notification to the equivalent authorities in Victoria for their assessment and review.

It was the mother’s evidence that while in that city, she spent time at the hotel pool and visited the beach with the child. She attempted to salvage the child’s holiday.
On 15 April 2026, the father filed his application. The matter was listed for urgent hearing because, from the father’s material, it appeared that the child was in the care of the child protection authorities .
The mother and child returned to Melbourne.
The hearing proceeded before me at 11:00am on 16 April 2026. The hearing was briefly adjourned to provide the father with time to read the mother’s material filed and served upon him electronically that morning. It subsequently proceeded on the basis that the father was only seeking orders for the mother to undertake hair follicle testing.
That day, I ordered that the father’s application be dismissed, and the mother’s costs in relation to this application be reserved until the delivery of these reasons for judgment.
The substantive proceeding is next listed for case management hearing before Justice Johns on 30 April 2026. Both parties have attended upon Dr C for psychiatric assessment, whose report is due to be provided in late April 2026. The family report of Dr B is due to be provided in or around June 2026.

CONSIDERATION

The issue for consideration before me is the father’s application for the mother to undergo hair follicle testing.
Pursuant to s 102NJ(1)(c) of the Act, the Court has the power to make an order in relation to an issue arising out of the proceedings, including for a party to undergo drug and/or alcohol testing without their consent.
The father has repeatedly sought orders for the mother to undertake hair follicle testing throughout the wider proceedings. The wife has refused to undertake such testing voluntarily, submitting that there is no proper basis for her to do so. She has never been ordered to undertake a hair follicle test.
In Justice John’s most recent judgment in this matter, delivered on 9 December 2025, she considered the father’s application for the mother to undertake hair follicle testing as follows:[6]
What remains to be determined is whether an additional order ought to be made for the mother to submit to hair follicle testing as sought by the father.
It was submitted on behalf of the mother that, firstly, the father adduces no evidence to support the making of such order. I accept that submission. There is no evidence before the Court that would justify the making of that order.
It was further submitted on behalf of the mother that the question of [X’s] residence with the mother is not an issue. Again, that is acknowledged on behalf of the father, and I have close regard to that in the circumstances of this case. The reality is that the mother’s role as primary parent is not subject to challenge. It is difficult to understand why such testing would be necessary or appropriate given that circumstance.
It was conceded on behalf of the mother that during the trial before Williams J, in the context of the threshold hearing, the mother was cross-examined as to her drug use. I am informed that, during the course of that cross-examination, the mother admitted that during the parties’ relationship, she did use illicit substances. It is her case that is no longer a feature of her life. It was submitted on behalf of the mother that the application made by the father late in the day without evidence to support it is a form of harassment, and that there is simply no reason for such order to be made. I accept that submission.
The submission made on behalf of the father is that, in circumstances where these proceedings have been on foot for some four years, and there have been some eight previous requests made on behalf of the father for the mother to submit to such testing, her agreement to do so would put the issue to rest. I do not consider that type of submission or argument to be basis for the type of intrusive order that is sought, and I do not consider that it would be an appropriate use of the Court’s power to accede to that application. Accordingly, that application will be dismissed.
The father’s position before me was substantially similar to that described by Justice Johns. I accept that in April 2026, the mother’s behaviour on board an aircraft resulted in a flight being diverted for her removal and arrest. The question of the mother’s behaviour on board the aircraft will be duly heard by the Magistrates’ Court. I accept that the child was consequently removed from the mother’s care overnight.
The father submitted that the mother’s behaviour on the aircraft “must – most likely – or maybe” was the result of substance abuse.[7] He asked that the Court infer that the mother was affected by substances from a 15 second video of a woman who is largely obscured from view being removed from an aircraft, played in full in court (Exhibit F1). He further submitted that he was concerned that the mother is “a druggie who’s leaving drugs around there and bringing people over there”, and that “the girl’s a junkie” with “drug addiction problems”.[8] He made a number of allegations from the bar table that did not appear in his affidavit and was repeatedly reminded of the scope of the hearing. He repeatedly offered to do a hair follicle test himself, despite counsel for the mother clearly stating there was no suggestion he required one.[9]
There is no evidence before the Court that the mother was affected by alcohol or illicit substances at the time of her arrest. The child’s residence with the mother is not an issue before me. Her role as the child’s primary caregiver is not subject to challenge.
The father has now made at least nine previous requests for hair follicle testing that have been refused. Counsel for the mother submitted that it is appropriate for the Court to again refuse the father’s application. I accept this submission.
The next court event before Justice Johns in the substantive proceeding is on 30 April 2026. The father, in his Application in a Proceeding filed 28 January 2026, sought substantially the same orders in relation to the mother undertaking hair follicle testing (at paragraphs 21 to 30). As a result, her Honour will shortly be hearing the same issue.
Counsel for the mother submitted that due to the nature of hair follicle testing, any current or recent drug usage on the part of the mother would be recorded in any test that Justice Johns might be minded to order. Further, counsel submitted that it is appropriate to await the recommendations of Dr C and Dr B before making such an order. I accept these submissions.

COSTS

Counsel for the wife sought for costs to be ordered against the father on a party-party basis in the fixed sum of $8,765 and tendered a table of costs on scale in support (now marked Exhibit M3). The father did not make any submissions in response in relation to an award of costs or any quantum.

Legal principles

Section 114UB(1) of the Act sets out the general rule that each party shall bear their own costs.
However, the Court, being satisfied that there are circumstances justifying it in doing so, has power pursuant to s 114UB(2) of the Act to make such order as to costs as the Court considers just. The formation of the opinion that there are justifying circumstances is followed by the Court’s exercise of discretionary power to make a costs order.
In considering what (if any) order for costs should be made, the Court shall have regard, relevantly, to the matters in s 114UB(3) of the Act which are as follows:
(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;
(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

Although the Court must have regard to all the matters in s 114UB(3) of the Act, each matter’s particular relevance will depend upon the circumstances of the case. The matters are not to be read in a restrictive way and the discretion afforded to the Court is a broad one.[10] No one factor in s 114UB(3) is determinative, and the Court may give such weigh as it considers relevant to any factor. In Fitzgerald (as child representative for A (Legal Aid Commission of Tasmania)) v Fish and Another [2005] FamCA 158; (2005) 33 Fam LR 123 at 130, the Full Court observed:
...Nowhere in subs (2A) [now subs (3)] or elsewhere in s 117 [now s 114UB], is there any prescription that more than one factor must be present before an order for costs is made nor of comparative weight of the factors set out in subs (2A) [now subs (3)]. As a consequence, there is nothing to prevent any factor being the sole foundation for an order for costs.
I turn below to a brief consideration of the circumstances and/or circumstance that would justify the Court making an order for costs in the exercise of my broad discretion in accordance with s 114UB(2). Those considerations not referred to are not relevant on the facts of this case but have been examined by me.

Section 114UB(3)(a) the financial circumstances of each of the parties

The mother is a health professional, earning approximately $1,800 per week. She lives in a rental property and has primary care of the child. She deposed to owing over $600,000 in legal fees.
Counsel for the mother submits that I should accept that the father is a wealthy man of means, despite his claims not to own or control F Pty Ltd (itself valued somewhere between $100-200 million). His most recent Costs Notice, filed 9 December 2025, reflects legal costs to that date of over $1.3 million, of which $68,205.65 was owing and the rest had been paid.
The father’s role in relation to F Pty Ltd is a matter for final hearing. I accept, however, counsel’s submission that the father has access to funds sufficient to pay the mother’s fixed costs.

Section 114UB(3)(c) conduct of the parties

The father’s conduct in filing an Application in a Proceeding seeking the orders that he did, and claiming the matter was urgent, in circumstances where the child had returned to her primary carer’s care the next day, a matter he could easily have ascertained, was conduct which was opportunistic; a waste of the public resources of the Court in the context of the history of the litigation and the impending listing before the Honourable Justice Johns; and occasioned unnecessary costs to be incurred by the wife.

Section 114UB(3)(e) whether any party has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings

The father brought the application in this proceeding and was wholly unsuccessful.
Given the above, I shall order the father pay the mother’s costs in the fixed sum as claimed, the father making no submissions when given the opportunity.
I certify that the preceding sixty-two (62) numbered paragraphs are a true copy of the Reasons for Judgment of the Honourable Justice Hartnett.

Associate:

Dated: 24 April 2026
